chunk_summary: This section focuses on responsive feeding counseling and behavior change strategies, emphasizing practical, culturally sensitive approaches to improve mealtime behaviors, reduce conflict, and promote healthy dietary habits in children.
learning_objectives: Understand the principles of responsive feeding, including recognizing hunger and satiety cues.
Learn strategies to reduce mealtime conflict and encourage healthy eating behaviors.
Apply motivational interviewing (MI) techniques to address caregiver concerns and set achievable goals.
Incorporate culturally appropriate feeding practices and tools into counseling sessions.
key_facts: Meals should follow a predictable routine with a duration of 20–30 minutes, stopping without punishment.
Hunger cues include reaching, opening the mouth, and leaning forward, while fullness cues include turning away, slowing, and pushing the spoon.
New foods should be introduced through repeated neutral exposure, often requiring 8–10 separate offerings.
Toddlers who graze should have planned snacks and avoid constant milk or juice to prevent appetite suppression.
Motivational interviewing (MI) can help caregivers set one achievable change for the next 2 weeks, with follow-up visits to reinforce progress.
reference_quote: UNICEF IYCF counseling cards and national programs (including MAA for breastfeeding support) provide pictorial tools that can be used in clinic to model responsive behaviors and reduce blame.
